title: The Director of Public Prosecutions v. Kydero Richardson; Kydero Richardson v. The Director of Public Prosecutions
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCrApp No. 205 of 2024
B E T W E E N
THE DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
Appellant
AND
KYDERO RICHARDSON
Respondent
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCrApp No. 214 of 2024
B E T W E E N
KYDERO RICHARDSON
Appellant
AND
THE DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
Respondent
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Turner, JA
APPEARANCES: Ms. Davina Pinder, Counsel for the Appellant in No. 205
Mr. Ryszard Humes, Counsel for the Respondent in No. 205
Mr. Ryszard Humes, Counsel for the Appellant in No. 214
Ms. Davina Pinder, Counsel for the Respondent in No. 214
DATES: 4 November 2025; 23 February 2026; 17 April 2026
********************************************

Criminal Appeal – Murder – Manslaughter - Appeal against conviction – Appeal against sentence
- Section 290 (2)(c) of the Penal Code - Section 291 (1)(b) of the Penal Code – Alleged misdirection
of trial judge - Record of Interview and Caution statement - Failure to leave defence of accident -
Failure to direct jury on provocation - Failure to leave the alternative verdict of manslaughter -
Whether sentence unduly lenient
On 7 February 2021, a physical altercation occurred between the deceased and Kydero Richardson
(“the Appellant” or “Richardson”). The incident began at Second Street and Palm Tree Avenue
when the deceased nearly struck the Appellant’s girlfriend while driving, leading to a verbal
exchange. The deceased stopped his vehicle, and the Appellant joined the argument. Despite
attempts by the deceased’s girlfriend and the Appellant’s sister to intervene, the situation escalated
after the deceased spat on the Appellant’s girlfriend, resulting in a fight involving several persons.
During the altercation, the deceased sustained three stab wounds and later died from his injuries.
The Appellant was arrested that evening and, during a police interview, admitted being involved in
the fight and that a knife was present during the brawl, but denied stabbing the deceased.
The Appellant was charged on 9 February 2021 with Murder contrary to sections 290(2)(c) and
291(1)(b) of the Penal Code, Chapter 84, and was arraigned on 12 February 2021 before the
Magistrate’s Court. The trial commenced before the Supreme Court, and on 8 March 2024, a nine-
member jury returned a unanimous verdict of guilty of murder. On 12 November 2024, the
Appellant was sentenced to twenty (20) years’ imprisonment at the Bahamas Department of
Correctional Services.
The Director of Public Prosecutions appealed against the sentence, and the Appellant appealed
against his conviction. The Director of Public Prosecutions argued that the sentence passed by the
learned trial judge was unduly lenient and Richardson argued that the learned trial judge misdirected
the jury and failed to leave the issues of manslaughter, provocation, and accident for the jury’s
consideration.
Held: Appeal against conviction allowed. The conviction for murder was set aside and substituted
with manslaughter and a sentence of twenty (20) years’ imprisonment for manslaughter imposed.
No fault was found with the learned trial judge's directions to the jury. It was open to the jury to
consider the information contained in those statements and determine whether they amounted to a
confession. The jury was entitled to consider the statements “in the round” and determine what
weight should have been attached to the exculpatory portions. The learned trial judge was not
required to direct the jury on whether the statements were mixed statements. The jury was entitled
to determine the consequences of Richardson’s conduct when they assessed the evidence.
The learned trial judge did not err in failing to leave the defence of accident to the jury. There was
no evidence capable of supporting a finding that the stabbing was accidental.
Having regard to the circumstances of the case, it was appropriate to maintain a sentence of twenty
(20) years’ imprisonment, substituting the conviction for murder with a conviction for
manslaughter. Accordingly, the sentence fell within the appropriate range.
2

Andy Francis v. Regina SCCrApp No. 133 of 2009; considered.
Attorney General v. Larry Raymond Jones et al SCCrApp No.133 of 2009; applied.
R v Duncan (1981) 73 Cr App R 359; mentioned.
R v Sharp [1988] 1 All ER 65; mentioned.
Stephen Stubbs et al v Regina SCCr App No. 8 of 2014; applied.
_________________________________________________________________________________
R E A S O N S F O R D E C I S I O N
_________________________________________________________________________________
Delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P:
Introduction
1. These are two appeals that were heard and considered together.
2. The appeals are against the decision of Madam Justice Jeanine Weech-Gomez (“the learned trial
judge”) whereby on 8 March 2024, Richardson was convicted of murder contrary to sections 290
(2)(c) and 291(1)(b) of the Penal Code, Chapter 84. On 12 November 2024, the learned trial judge
imposed a sentence of twenty (20) years’ incarceration of the appellant at the Bahamas Department
of Correctional Services.
3. On 14 November 2024, the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions filed a Notice of Appeal
against sentence, and Richardson filed a Notice of Appeal against conviction on 28 November
2024, with an Amended Notice of Appeal filed on 30 October 2025.
4. On 23 February 2026, we heard the submissions and ordered that the conviction and sentence for
murder be set aside, substituting it with a conviction for manslaughter. After hearing submissions
on the appropriate sentence for manslaughter we determined that a twenty (20) year sentence was
appropriate for the offence of manslaughter. We gave brief reasons and indicated that more
detailed reasons would follow. These are our detailed written reasons.
Background Facts
5. On 7 February 2021, a physical altercation occurred between Jayvon Edwards (“the deceased”)
and the Appellant. The incident began at Second Street and Palm Tree Avenue when the deceased
nearly struck the Appellant’s girlfriend while driving his vehicle. This led to a verbal exchange
between the deceased and the Appellant’s girlfriend, after which the deceased stopped and parked
his vehicle. The Appellant then joined the argument.
6. The deceased’s girlfriend and Appellant’s sister attempted to intervene and stop the altercation.
Despite their efforts, the argument escalated when the deceased spat on the Appellant’s girlfriend.
This resulted in a fight between the Appellant and the deceased, during which several others
became involved.
3

7. During the altercation, the deceased was stabbed three times: once to the right posterior area of the
neck, once to the right side of the upper back, and another to the left thigh. He was transported to
the hospital by private vehicle, but he later died from his injuries. Evidence presented at trial
indicated that the Appellant was arrested later that same evening and was interviewed by the police.
During the interview, he admitted that he had been involved in a fight with the deceased and stated
that a knife was present during the brawl, but he denied stabbing the deceased.
8. The Appellant was charged on 9 February 2021 with murder, contrary to sections 290 (2)(c) and
291(1)(b) of the Penal Code, Chapter 84, and on 12 February 2021, the Appellant was arraigned
before Magistrate’s Court No. 8.
9. Trial commenced on 21 February 2021 before the Supreme Court, and the Appellant was convicted
on 8 March 2024, where the nine-member jury returned a unanimous verdict of guilty for the
offence of murder.
10. On 12 November 2024, the Appellant was sentenced to twenty (20) years at the Bahamas
Department of Correctional Services.
11. The Appellant challenged his conviction, and the Director of Public Prosecutions challenged the
sentence imposed by the learned trial judge.
12. We have considered Richardson’s appeal against his conviction. He has four (4) grounds, and
considered them in turn.
RICHARDSON’S GROUNDS OF APPEAL
13. Richardson filed a Notice of Appeal on 28 November 2024, and an Amended Notice of Appeal
was filed on 30 October 2025. The aforesaid Amended Notice contained the following grounds of
appeal:
“1. The Learned Trial Judge erred in law and in fact when she
misdirected the jury on the evidence regarding the Appellant’s
Record of Interview and Statement under Caution. The Learned
Judge gave the following directions: -
a. That the contents of the Appellant’s Record of Interview and
Statement under caution did not amount to a confession.
b. However, if the jury found that the Appellants Record of
Interview and Statement under Caution did amount to a
confession then the jury would have to assess whether it was true
and reliable.
c. If the jury found that the confession was true and reliable, then
they must treat it as evidence which supports the prosecution’s
case.
4

d. If the jury found that the Confession was not true then the jury
should ignore it all together.
2. The Learned trial judge erred by not leaving the alternative
verdict of manslaughter to the jury.
3. The Learned Trial Judge erred by failing to direct the jury on
the issue of provocation.
4. The Learned Trial Judge erred by failing to leave the defence
of accident to the jury.”
14. At the beginning of the hearing, Counsel for the Respondent indicated that she was prepared to
accede to grounds two (2) and three (3) and would accept an order that the murder conviction be
set aside and substituted with one of manslaughter.
15. Counsel for the Appellant, although agreeing that the Murder conviction should be set aside,
contended that the proper order should be that a retrial be held. He was of the view that a jury,
properly directed, at a new trial could potentially acquit on the basis of accident or self-defence.
16. The Appellant’s complaints under these grounds are whether the learned trial judge misdirected
the jury regarding whether the Appellant’s Record of Interview and the Statement under Caution
amounted to a confession, and whether the learned trial judge erred in failing to leave the defence
of accident to the jury.
Ground One - Misdirection on the Appellant’s Record of Interview and Statement under Caution
Ground Four – Learned trial judge’s failure to leave the defence of accident to the jury
17. Counsel for the Appellant, Mr. Humes, submitted that the learned judge erred when she
misdirected the jury on the evidence regarding Richardson’s Record of Interview and Statement
under Caution. Counsel suggested that the learned trial judge ought to have properly directed the
jury, as a matter of law, whether Richardson’s statement constituted a confession. This omission,
he argued, supported the prosecution’s position rather than what Richardson had actually stated.
In support of this submission, counsel referred to page 233 of the transcript, which contains the
learned trial judge’s direction to the jury on the issue of confession:
“...In this case though, although the defendant did not give
evidence, the defendant did say certain things in his statement.
They do not however amount, Madam Forewomen and members
of the jury, to a confession. And when you come to decide, if you
can safely rely upon the evidence, you must consider the
defendant's explanation of having said what he said and did, and
the interpretation you are invited to put on that. If you're not
sure what the defendant says in anyway amounts to confession,
5

then you should take no account of it [...]. If, however, in your
opinion, the statement does amount to a confession, then you
need to question whether it is true and reliable. If you are sure
that it is true and/or reliable, then you must treat it as evidence
which supports the prosecution's case. If you are not sure it is
true, you should ignore it all together.”
18. Counsel further submitted that there was a discussion between the learned trial judge and counsel
who represented Richardson during the trial regarding whether Richardson’s statement amounted
to a confession. At page 237, lines 3-11 of the transcript, the following exchange states:
“[COUNSEL]: Yes, my Lady.
Notwithstanding the question my Learned Friend has just asked,
I just want to be clear that in your consideration, that you
reminded the jury to consider, I was not quite sure how you said
it but was it your observation or your consideration when
reminding them, that the statement of Mr. Kydero Richardson,
does not amount to a confession?
THE COURT: It does not. I said that.”
19. As for the defence of accident, counsel submitted that the learned trial judge erred in failing to
direct the jury on that defence or to leave it for their consideration, which, he contends, arose from
the contents of Richardson’s Record of Interview. Counsel argued that the deceased, Edwards, was
stabbed accidentally during a struggle for the knife while Richardson was attempting to prevent
himself from being stabbed by the deceased. The Appellant’s Record of Interview at page 91 of
the transcript states:
“He stated, “I didn't have a knife. It was his knife. We got into a
scuffle and while we was fighting, to avoid me getting stab with
his knife, he ended up getting stab.”
So I asked him if he took the knife from Jayvon. And he said,
“Me and him had the knife.”
20. Counsel argued that the jury may have been left confused as to how the appellant’s Record of
Interview and Statement under Caution should have been treated moments before the nine-member
jury delivered its verdict. In those circumstances, counsel therefore contended that the conviction
be quashed and the matter be remitted for a retrial on the basis that the learned trial judge
misdirected the jury in this regard.
21. Further, Counsel submitted that based on the contents of Richardson’s Statement under Caution,
the jury ought to have been properly directed on the defence of accident. The contents of the
statement raise the possibility that Richardson did not intentionally stab the deceased during the
6

struggle between himself and the deceased. Counsel placed reliance on the Statement under
Caution found at page 98 of the transcript, which states:
“As we came at each other people came towards us and we
dropped on the floor. And as we was hassling with the knife
that's when he got stab, because I was trying to prevent me
getting stab. We continue fighting, then someone grab me and
tell me let's go home. When I look back the fight was still
going on. That it.”
22. In further support of his submission above, counsel relied on Alexander Von Stark v Regina
[2000] 4 LRC, which addresses the responsibility and function of a judge to place before the jury
all possible conclusions arising from the evidence presented at trial by both parties, in order to
ensure that the case reaches a just and sound conclusion. Counsel argued that, in the present case,
the learned trial judge's failure to include the defence of accident in her summation to the jury
deprived the jury of an opportunity to consider whether Richardson would have been convicted of
the lesser offence of manslaughter.
23. In response, counsel for the Respondent, Ms. Pinder, submitted that the learned trial judge did not
err when she gave the direction to the jury on the issue of confession. She contended that
Richardson’s Record of Interview and Statement, taken under Caution, contained both inculpatory
and exculpatory statements that placed him at the scene of the incident. Counsel further argued
that Richardson admitted to having the knife in his possession after taking it from the deceased
and indirectly admitted that, while scuffling with the deceased, he stabbed him with the knife. In
support of this submission, counsel relied on the Appellant’s Record of Interview found at page
107 of the transcript, which states:
“Okay. He said, “I didn’t had a knife, it was his knife. And we
got into a scuffle. And as we was fighting to avoid me getting stab
with his knife, he end up getting stab.”
24. To add to Counsel’s point above, she argued that the statements Richardson made in his Record
of Interview and in his Statement under Caution constituted a mixed statement which contained
both inculpatory and exculpatory elements. In her written submissions, she referred this Court to
section 20(5) of the Evidence Act, Chapter 65, which defines a “confession” and added that a
confession may include a mixed statement which is partly adverse to its maker. Section 20 (5)
provides:
“20(5) In this Act — “confession” includes any statement wholly
or partly adverse to the person who made it, whether made to a
person in authority or not and whether made in words or
otherwise;...” [Emphasis added]
25. Counsel relied on guidance from authorities such as R v Sharp [1988] 1 All ER 65, R v Duncan
(1981) 73 Cr App R 359, and Stephen Stubbs et al v Regina SCCr App No. 8 of 2014, which
indicate that where an accused makes a statement that contains both incriminating and exculpatory
7

elements, the statement is to be treated as a mixed statement and must be admitted in its entirety,
leaving the jury to determine the weight that should be attached to the exculpatory portions of the
statement. In advancing this submission, counsel relied on the dicta of Conteh, JA, in Stubbs
(above), where he stated:
“...where a mixed statement is under consideration, the whole of
the statement, both the incriminating parts and the excuses must
be left to the jury for their consideration.
…
When summing up the case to the jury the judge has a duty
remind the jury, in summary form, what the accused has said. It
will be sufficient if he identifies the central submissions and the
evidence that underpins them but the judge must generally
ensure that the jury receives a coherent rehearsal of the main
arguments that are being advanced by the accused. To determine
whether this was done properly and fairly the summing up must
be looked at in the round.” [Emphasis added]
26. Counsel further submitted that Richardson made inculpatory and exculpatory statements in his
Statement under Caution, which appears at pages 91 and 107 of the transcript (set out at paragraph
19). The transcript on page 98 also contains statements that reflect both elements. In particular,
Richardson appeared to have acknowledged that the deceased was stabbed during the course of a
struggle between them, while at the same time asserting that he was attempting to prevent himself
from being stabbed. Counsel relies on this submission, which is reflected in the transcript at page
98, lines 7 -13, which states:
“As we came at each other people came towards us and we
dropped on the floor. And as we was hassling with the knife that's
when he got stab, because I was trying to prevent me getting stab.
We continue fighting, then someone grab me and tell me let's go
home. When I look back the fight was still going on. That it.”
Counsel contended that these statements constituted a mixed statement which the jury ought to have
considered in its entirety.
27. In response to Mr. Humes submission that the learned trial judge failed to direct the jury on the
issue of accident, Ms. Pinder contended that any suggestion that Richardson stabbed the deceased
accidentally was without merit. She pointed to the evidence that the deceased sustained three stab
wounds, including to the back of his neck, to his thigh region and to his back. Counsel further
argued that Richardson's unsworn statements did not assert that the stabbing was accidental. In
addition, the Pathology Report prepared by Dr. Sands indicated that the fatal wound to the neck
was inflicted in a back-to-front motion, which was inconsistent with an accidental injury. Counsel
noted that the appellant never testified and therefore the only account before the jury was contained
in his unsworn statements admitted into evidence.
8

28. Having considered the submissions of both the Appellant and the Respondent, we found no fault
with the learned trial judge's directions to the jury on the issue as to whether the contents of the
Appellant’s Record of Interview and Statement under Caution amounted to a confession. We do
not accept Mr. Humes’ contention. At a retrial, there is a presumption that the learned trial judge
will give a proper direction to the jury on the contents of the statement and what constitutes a
confession. It was open to the jury to consider the information contained in those statements and
determine whether they amounted to a confession. In Stubbs, this Court found that, “where a
mixed statement is under consideration, the whole of the statement… must be left to the
jury.” Applying that principle to the present case, in our judgment, Richardson’s statements
contained both inculpatory and exculpatory elements which placed him on the scene of the
incident. The jury was entitled to consider the statements “in the round” and determine what
weight should have been attached to the exculpatory portions.
29. Further, the learned trial judge was not required to direct the jury on whether the statements were
mixed statements. The statements indicated that Richardson and the deceased were involved in a
fight during which the deceased was stabbed. The stabbing occurred in the course of the unlawful
fight, to which the jury was entitled to determine the consequences of Richardson’s conduct when
they assessed the evidence.
30. With respect to the defence of accident, we are also satisfied that the learned trial judge did not err
in failing to leave the defence of accident to the jury. We accept Ms. Pinder’s submission that there
was no evidence capable of supporting a finding that Richardson accidentally stabbed the
deceased. The medical evidence from the Pathology Report of Dr. Sands indicated that the
deceased sustained three stab wounds, including a fatal wound to the neck, the nature and location
of which were inconsistent with an accidental injury. The statements of the deceased, coupled with
the amount of times the deceased was stabbed by Richardson and the location of the stab wounds,
each pointed to the stabbing not being accidental. Further, Richardson’s statements did not suggest
that the stabbing occurred by accident but rather indicated that he and the deceased were engaged
in a fight during which both had possession of a knife. In those circumstances, the defence of
accident did not properly arise on the evidence, and there was no evidential basis upon which it
could properly have been left to the jury.
31. Accordingly, in our judgment, both grounds held no merit and were dismissed.
Ground Two – Learned trial judge’s failure to leave manslaughter to the jury
Ground Three – Learned trial judge’s failure to direct jury on provocation
32. Grounds two (2) and three (3) overlapped and were therefore considered together.
33. Mr. Humes’ contention was that the learned trial judge failed to direct the jury on manslaughter
and provocation. In response, Ms. Pinder conceded that the learned trial judge had erred in this
regard.
9

34. We agreed with Ms. Pinder’s concession. In those circumstances, had the learned trial judge
directed the jury on manslaughter and provocation, there would have been evidence upon which
the jury could properly have returned a different verdict.
35. In the circumstances, it was unnecessary for us to address both grounds in any further detail.
Accordingly, in our judgment, both grounds held merit and were allowed. As a result of our
finding, that the issues of accident and self defence did not properly arise on the evidence and that
there was no evidential basis from which those issues could have properly been left to the jury, we
were satisfied that there was no need to remit the matter to the Supreme Court for a retrial. It was
also our considered view that this Court was well placed to determine the appropriate sentence for
Manslaughter. We therefore allowed the appeal, quashed the conviction for murder and substituted
a conviction for manslaughter.
The DPP’s appeal against sentence
36. Ms. Pinder, Counsel for The Director of Public Prosecutions, filed their Notice of Appeal on 14
November 2024 challenging the twenty (20) year sentence imposed by the learned trial judge.
There was no need to consider this appeal as it was rendered moot by the quashing of the murder
conviction. We instead heard the parties on the question of the appropriate sentence for the
manslaughter conviction.
37. Ms. Pinder’s submission was that a sentence of twenty-five (25) years would be appropriate in the
circumstances. She relied on Andy Francis v. Regina SCCrApp No. 133 of 2009 and argued that
the applicable sentencing range for manslaughter is twenty-five (25) to thirty (30) years,
particularly in cases involving an unlawful fight, and that Richardson should be sentenced within
that range.
38. On the other hand, Mr. Humes’ submission was that if this Court were to substitute the murder
conviction for manslaughter, the sentence should not be disturbed. In those circumstances, he
contended that the sentence of twenty (20) years was appropriate, as it fell within the range for the
offence of manslaughter and would not have been unduly lenient.
39. Having considered the submissions advanced by both counsel, we considered that the appropriate
sentence for manslaughter that this Court should impose is twenty (20) years. In determining the
appropriate sentence for manslaughter, we relied on the dicta of the Rt. Hon Dame Sawyer, P (as
she then was) in Attorney General v. Larry Raymond Jones et al SCCrApp Nos. 12, 18 and 19
of 2007, where she stated:
“15. …It must be noted that over the past 7 years, this court
has set guidelines in respect of persons convicted of
manslaughter. Sentences passed by this court during that
period range from 18 years to 35 years imprisonment,
bearing in mind the character of the convicted person, the
circumstances in which the offence was committed and
whether the convicted person showed any remorse for the
killing (e.g., by pleading guilty at the earliest opportunity) to
10

name some of the usual considerations to be taken into
account by the sentencing judge.
16. We accept that some cases in which accused person are
convicted of manslaughter instead of murder are as heinous
as some murders but they are able to bring themselves within
one or more of the partial excuses in the Penal Code such as
provocation, diminished responsibility or excessive self-
defence, to name a few.” [Emphasis added]
40. Similarly, in Andy Francis v. Regina (above), this Court imposed a sentence of twenty-five (25)
years for manslaughter, which arose from a stabbing incident bearing similar facts to those of the
present case, which this Court considered to be within the appropriate sentencing range.
41. We note that there have been cases where sentences for manslaughter have fallen even below 18
years and it is clear that each case must be considered on its particular facts. Having regard to the
circumstances of the case, we considered it appropriate to maintain a sentence of twenty (20) years’
imprisonment, substituting the conviction for murder with a conviction for manslaughter as
submitted by Mr. Humes. In our view, the sentence was appropriate and fell within the appropriate
range.
Disposition
42. As a result, for the foregoing reasons, the appeal against conviction was allowed. The conviction
for murder was set aside and substituted with manslaughter and a sentence of twenty (20) years’
imprisonment for manslaughter was imposed.
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
__________________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
__________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Turner, JA
11
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas

summary: Criminal Appeal – Murder – Manslaughter - Appeal against conviction – Appeal against sentence - Section 290 (2)(c) of the Penal Code - Section 291 (1)(b) of the Penal Code – Alleged misdirection of trial judge - Record of Interview and Caution statement - Failure to leave defence of accident - Failure to direct jury on provocation - Failure to leave the alternative verdict of manslaughter - Whether sentence unduly lenient
